en.extract:
The black-faced cormorant (Phalacrocorax fuscescens), also known as the black-faced shag, is a medium-sized member of the cormorant family. Upperparts, including facial skin and bill, are black, with white underparts. It is endemic to coastal regions of southern Australia.


Description

Like other cormorant species, the black-faced cormorant is a large aquatic bird, with a long hooked bill, webbed feet, and monochromatic plumage. This is one of the largest cormorants found in south-western Australia and has pied plumage with the upper half of its body black and the undersides white. Its face is naked and black, hence the "black-faced" name, and the tail, feet, and thighs are also black. The back feathers are glossy, and its bill is dark grey with a prominent hook at the tip. It has blue-green eyes. When flying, it holds its head level or lower than its body and holds its wings in a cross-shape like most cormorants.
Species that are similar in appearance include the pied cormorant (slightly larger) and the little pied cormorant (slightly smaller).


Plumages
The breeding and non-breeding plumages are very similar, with the formation of fine white streaks along the neck during breeding until eggs are laid. Juveniles have dark-brown upper feathers, including dark ear coverts and a dark face, with a lighter underside streaked with brown. Following the Juvenile plumage, black-faced cormorants have immature plumage which are similar to adult plumage.


Sexual Dimorphism

Black-faced cormorants have very subtle sexual dimorphism, making it difficult to differentiate sexes in the field. In general, males are larger and more robust than females, who are more slender in comparison. Males also have larger bills than females, with male bills measuring greater than 77 millimeters and female bills less than 76 mm.


Vocalization
Black-faced cormorants are usually silent when away from the breeding colony. However, when they are at their nests, the male will emit a loud honk or guttural croak, and the female will hiss when approached.


Taxonomy
The black-faced cormorant is one of around 40 species in the cormorant and shag family Phalacrocoracidae. This family split off from the darter family Anhingadae over 40 million years ago, so it has a relatively independent evolutionary history. Of the suggested 7 genera in the cormorant family, the black-faced cormorant is part of the Old-Word cormorants Phalacrocorax. This genus diverged from its sister genus, the North-Pacific Cormorants Urile around 10 million years ago.
The closest related species to the black-faced cormorant is the pied cormorant P. varius.


Habitat and Distribution
Black-faced cormorants are endemic to the coastal regions of southern Australia and Tasmania. The population is estimated to in the tens of thousands, and is distributed across Western Australia, South Australia, Victoria and Tasmania, particularly in the Bass Strait and Spencer Gulf. There are two independent populations: one on the southern coast of Western Australia, and the other on the coasts of Southern Australia, Victoria and Tasmania.


Habitat

Unlike the other cormorants found around the Australian continent, the habitat of the black-faced cormorant is exclusively coastal and marine. They can be found in coastal waters, inlets, rocky shores, and offshore islands. Occasionally black-faced cormorants can be found in estuaries of rivers. Colonies are usually found on steep cliffs and rocky islands where they nest. In the winter, black-faced cormorants leave coastal waters to breed in these colonies.


Behaviour and Ecology
Because the black-faced cormorant is an exclusively marine bird, its behaviours enable it to be well-adapted to its environment. It uses pursuit-diving to capture fish, and nests high on cliffs to avoid predators.


Diet
The black-faced cormorant eats fish almost exclusively. Its diet generally consists of benthic or demersal fish, as well as some cephalopods, and varies between breeding and non-breeding seasons. It forages along the coast and at the mouths of rivers, and will dive up to 12 m to catch its prey. After diving, the black-faced cormorant will spread its wings to dry its feathers in the sun. Black-faced cormorants will occasionally forage together in flocks. While breeding, its preferred prey items are wrasse and silver trevally. After the breeding season, silverbelly, wrasse, whiting and hardyheads are consumed most frequently. Prey is consumed whole, and non-digestible parts are regurgitated in pellets.


Reproduction
Breeding cormorants nest in pairs or breeding colonies on islands or cliffs. They are likely monogamous like other cormorant species. Nesting sites are on bare rocks, often close to water, and are solidly built of driftwood, seaweed, and other plants with a 35–45 cm diameter. 2 to 3 elongated oval eggs are then laid. The eggs are pale blue-green and measure 58 x 36 mm.
The breeding season for black-faced cormorants varies between populations, and has been observed to occur anywhere from June to February. In the Bass Strait, the black-faced cormorant breeds from June to September, with a peak laying date around the end of July. The reason for this may be to avoid hot summer temperatures that can affect chicks. However, colonies in western and southern Australia breed later in the year, from September to February.


Threats
Black-faced cormorants are considered 'Least Concern' by the IUCN, due to their large range and populations. In Australia, they are considered 'Secure' federally as well as in South Australia, Western Australia, and Tasmania. However, in Victoria, the black-faced cormorant is considered 'Vulnerable'. Breeding colonies are very sensitive to human presences, so it is important that important breeding habitats are protected from disruption.


References


External links
BirdLife International (2006) Species factsheet: Phalacrocorax fuscescens. Downloaded from https://web.archive.org/web/20210828092113/https://www.birdlife.org/ on 14 November 2006
Johnsgaard, P. A. (1993).  Cormorants, Darters, and Pelicans of the World.  Washington: Smithsonian Institution Press.  ISBN 1-56098-216-0
Marchant, S.; & Higgins, P.J. (Coordinators). (2000). Handbook of Australian, New Zealand and Antarctic Birds. Vol.1: Ratites to Ducks. Oxford University Press: Melbourne. ISBN 978-0-19-553068-1

[[File:Phalacrocorax fuscescens Roosting.jpg|thumb|Roosting, [[Bruny Island]]]]
[[File:Phalacrocorax fuscescens - Marion Bay.jpg|thumb|In flight]]

The '''black-faced cormorant''' ('''''Phalacrocorax fuscescens'''''), also known as the '''black-faced shag''', is a medium-sized member of the [[cormorant]] family. Upperparts, including facial skin and bill, are black, with white underparts. It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to coastal regions of southern Australia.

== Description ==
[[File:Phalacrocorax fuscescens wineglass.jpg|alt=A bird with dark-brown feathers on the upper body, and white under body slightly streaked.|thumb|A black-faced cormorant with juvenile plummage.]]
Like other cormorant species, the black-faced cormorant is a large aquatic bird, with a long hooked bill, webbed feet, and monochromatic plumage.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|last1=R.|first1=Taylor, Andrew|last2=Peter|first2=Dann|last3=P.Y.|first3=Arnould, John|date=2013-01-01|title=Timing of breeding and diet of the Black-faced cormorant Phalacrocorax fuscescens|journal=Marine Ornithology|language=en|volume=41|issue=1|doi=10.5038/2074-1235.41.1.1003 |issn=1018-3337|hdl=10536/DRO/DU:30057640}}</ref><ref name=":4">{{Cite journal|last1=Kennedy|first1=Martyn|last2=Spencer|first2=Hamish G.|title=Classification of the cormorants of the world|journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution|volume=79|pages=249–257|doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2014.06.020|pmid=24994028|year=2014 |bibcode=2014MolPE..79..249K }}</ref> This is one of the largest cormorants found in south-western Australia<ref name=":0" /> and has [[pied]] plumage with the upper half of its body black and the undersides white. Its face is naked and black, hence the "black-faced" name, and the tail, feet, and thighs are also black.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|title=Field guide to Australian birds|last=K.|first=Morcombe, Michael|date=2003|publisher=Steve Parish Pub|isbn=978-1-74021-417-9|edition=2nd ed., rev. and updated|location=Archerfield, Qld.|oclc=55632177}}</ref> The back feathers are glossy, and its bill is dark grey with a prominent hook at the tip. It has blue-green eyes. When flying, it holds its head level or lower than its body and holds its wings in a cross-shape like most cormorants.<ref name=":1" />

Species that are similar in appearance include the [[pied cormorant]] (slightly larger) and the [[little pied cormorant]] (slightly smaller).<ref name=Marchant1990>{{Cite book|title=Handbook of Australian, New Zealand & Antarctic Birds. Vol. 1, Ratites to ducks.|last1=Marchant|first1=S|last2=Higgins|first2=P J|publisher=Oxford University Press|year=1990|location=Melbourne}}</ref>

=== Plumages ===
The breeding and non-breeding plumages are very similar, with the formation of fine white streaks along the neck during breeding until eggs are laid.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|url=http://www.parks.tas.gov.au/?base=19290|title=Parks & Wildlife Service – Black-faced Cormorant, Phalacrocorax fuscescens|website=parks.tas.gov.au|access-date=2017-10-14}}</ref> Juveniles have dark-brown upper feathers, including dark [[Covert feather|ear coverts]] and a dark face, with a lighter underside streaked with brown. Following the Juvenile plumage, black-faced cormorants have immature plumage which are similar to adult plumage.<ref name=":1" />

=== Sexual Dimorphism ===
[[File:Phalacrocorax fuscescens - Derwent River Estuary crop2.jpg|alt=A black-faced cormorant sits on a rock. Its breeding plumage has fine white streaks on its black neck.|thumb|Black-faced cormorant in breeding plumage with fine white streaking on the back of the neck.]]
Black-faced cormorants have very subtle [[sexual dimorphism]], making it difficult to differentiate sexes in the field. In general, males are larger and more robust than females, who are more slender in comparison.<ref name=":3">{{Cite journal|last1=Riordan|first1=Julie|last2=Johnston|first2=Greg|date=2013-03-01|title=Morphological Sex Determination in Black-Faced Cormorants (Phalacrocorax fuscescens)|journal=Waterbirds|volume=36|issue=1|pages=94–101|doi=10.1675/063.036.0114|s2cid=84519074|issn=1524-4695}}</ref> Males also have larger [[Beak|bills]] than females, with male bills measuring greater than 77 millimeters and female bills less than 76 mm.<ref name=":3" />

=== Vocalization ===
Black-faced cormorants are usually silent when away from the breeding colony.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":3" /><ref name=":2" /> However, when they are at their nests, the male will emit a loud honk or guttural croak, and the female will hiss when approached.<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" />
{{Birdsong
 | url = http://www.xeno-canto.org/species/Phalacrocorax-fuscescens
 | species = Black-faced cormorant
 | caption song1 = Several black-faced cormorants calling in a colony
|image=Black_faced_cormorant.jpg}}

== Taxonomy ==
The black-faced cormorant is one of around 40 species in the cormorant and shag family [[Cormorant|Phalacrocoracidae]].<ref name=":4" /> This family split off from the darter family [[Darter|Anhingadae]] over 40 million years ago, so it has a relatively independent evolutionary history.<ref name=":4" /> Of the suggested 7 genera in the cormorant family,<ref name=":4" /> the black-faced cormorant is part of the Old-Word cormorants ''Phalacrocorax.'' This genus diverged from its [[Sister group|sister]] genus, the North-Pacific Cormorants ''Urile'' around 10 million years ago.<ref name=":4" />

The closest related species to the black-faced cormorant is the [[Australian pied cormorant|pied cormorant]] ''P. varius''.<ref name=":4" />

==  Habitat and Distribution  ==
Black-faced cormorants are [[Endemism|endemic]] to the coastal regions of southern Australia and Tasmania.<ref name=":5">{{Cite journal|last1=Melrose|first1=W.D.|last2=Nicol|first2=S.C.|title=Haematology, red cell metabolism and blood chemistry of the black-faced cormorant Leucocarbo fuscescens|journal=Comparative Biochemistry and Physiology Part A: Physiology|volume=102|issue=1|pages=67–70|doi=10.1016/0300-9629(92)90013-g|year=1992|pmid=1351826}}</ref> The population is estimated to in the tens of thousands,<ref name="iucn status 11 November 2021" /> and is distributed across [[Western Australia]], [[South Australia]], [[Victoria (Australia)|Victoria]] and [[Tasmania]], particularly in the [[Bass Strait]] and [[Spencer Gulf]].<ref name=":7">{{Cite web|url=http://birdlife.org.au/bird-profile/black-faced-cormorant|title=Black-faced Cormorant {{!}} BirdLife Australia|website=birdlife.org.au|language=en|access-date=2017-10-15}}</ref> There are two independent populations: one on the southern coast of Western Australia, and the other on the coasts of Southern Australia, Victoria and Tasmania.<ref name="iucn status 11 November 2021" />

=== Habitat ===
[[File:Australia South Coast.jpg|thumb|Rocky cliffs on the southern coast of Australia.]]
Unlike the other cormorants found around the Australian continent, the habitat of the black-faced cormorant is exclusively [[coast]]al and [[Marine habitats|marine]].<ref name=":3" /> They can be found in coastal waters, [[inlet]]s, rocky shores, and offshore islands.<ref name=":7" /> Occasionally black-faced cormorants can be found in [[Estuary|estuaries]] of rivers.<ref name=":1" /> Colonies are usually found on steep cliffs and rocky islands where they nest.<ref name=":0" /> In the winter, black-faced cormorants leave coastal waters to breed in these colonies.<ref name=":0" />

== Behaviour and Ecology  ==
Because the black-faced cormorant is an exclusively marine bird, its behaviours enable it to be well-adapted to its environment. It uses [[Seabird|pursuit-diving]] to capture fish,<ref name="iucn status 11 November 2021" /> and nests high on cliffs<ref name=":0" /> to avoid predators.

=== Diet ===
The black-faced cormorant eats fish almost exclusively. Its diet generally consists of [[Benthic zone|benthic]] or [[Demersal fish|demersal]] fish, as well as some [[cephalopod]]s, and varies between breeding and non-breeding seasons.<ref name=":0" /> It forages along the coast and at the mouths of rivers,<ref name=":5" /> and will dive up to 12 m to catch its prey.<ref name=":7" /> After diving, the black-faced cormorant will spread its wings to dry its feathers in the sun.<ref name=":2" /> Black-faced cormorants will occasionally forage together in flocks.<ref name=":2" /> While breeding, its preferred prey items are [[wrasse]] and [[White trevally|silver trevally]].<ref name=":0" /> After the breeding season, [[Parequula melbournensis|silverbelly]], wrasse, [[Sillago|whiting]] and [[Old World silverside|hardyheads]] are consumed most frequently.<ref name=":0" /> Prey is consumed whole, and non-digestible parts are regurgitated in pellets.<ref name=":0" />

=== Reproduction ===
Breeding cormorants nest in pairs or breeding colonies on islands or cliffs.<ref name=":1" /> They are likely [[Monogamy in animals|monogamous]] like other cormorant species.<ref name=":3" /> Nesting sites are on bare rocks, often close to water, and are solidly built of driftwood, seaweed, and other plants with a 35–45 cm diameter.<ref name=":1" /> 2 to 3 elongated oval eggs are then laid.<ref name=":1" /> The eggs are pale blue-green and measure 58 x 36 mm.<ref name=":1" />

The breeding season for black-faced cormorants varies between populations, and has been observed to occur anywhere from June to February. In the Bass Strait, the black-faced cormorant breeds from June to September, with a peak laying date around the end of July.<ref name=":0" /> The reason for this may be to avoid hot summer temperatures that can affect chicks.<ref name=":0" /> However, colonies in western and southern Australia breed later in the year, from September to February.<ref name=":0" />

== Threats ==
Black-faced cormorants are considered 'Least Concern' by the [[IUCN Red List|IUCN]], due to their large range and populations.<ref name="iucn status 11 November 2021" /> In Australia, they are considered 'Secure' federally as well as in South Australia, Western Australia, and Tasmania.<ref name=":7" /> However, in Victoria, the black-faced cormorant is considered 'Vulnerable'.<ref name=":7" /> Breeding colonies are very sensitive to human presences,<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Robinson|first1=A. C.|last2=Armstrong|first2=D. M.|date=2017-01-02|title=An expedition to the Sir Joseph Banks Group of islands, South Australia: vertebrate fauna|journal=Transactions of the Royal Society of South Australia|volume=141|issue=1|pages=3–25|doi=10.1080/03721426.2016.1266572|bibcode=2017TRSAu.141....3R |s2cid=89367127|issn=0372-1426}}</ref><ref name=":0" /> so it is important that important breeding habitats are protected from disruption.[[File:Phalacrocorax fuscescens 1.jpg|thumb|Black-faced cormorants holding wings out to dry their feathers after diving.]]
